data.title: Chart Of The Day: Expanding Margins Help Fuel S&P Run
data.description:
By Mike Larson

At the "margin," US companies have never been more profitable. That’s a key reason the stock market keeps powering ahead!

Check out MoneyShow Chart of the Day, which comes courtesy of FactSet. It shows the blended net profit margin for companies in the S&P 500 Index (SPX) that have reported first-quarter results already. It’s currently at 13.4%. If that holds through the end of the Q1 earnings season, it’ll be the highest on record (the data goes back to 2009).

S&P Profit Margins are Hitting New Highs

It’s not a one-off anomaly, either. Profit margins have been climbing consistently since the end of 2023.

Information technology companies are reporting the biggest year-over-year margin expansion for Q1 - to 29.1% from 25.4% in Q1 2025. But four other sectors are also seeing margins climb, including financials and utilities. Analysts see S&P 500 margins expanding further to 14.6% by year-end.

So, if you’re wondering why stocks can hold up - in the face of lousy consumer sentiment or lingering war worries - just remember that on Wall Street, it’s all about profits. And right now, things look pretty good on that score.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

data.title: Third Avenue International Real Estate Value Fund Q1 2026 Letter
data.description:
Dear Fellow Shareholders,

We are pleased to provide you with the Third Avenue International Real Estate Value Fund (the “Fund”)(REIFX) report for the quarter ended March 31, 2026. The Fund delivered a return of -3.59% (after fees) for the quarter, outperforming the MSCI ACWI ex USA IMI Core Real Estate Index1, which returned -4.52% over the same period. The Fund’s outperformance was largely driven by its investments in Hong Kong-property companies.

Volatility stemming from the Iran conflict enabled the initiation of two new positions at deeply discounted prices, reflecting Fund Management’s focus on buying into well-financed entities with quality assets and management platforms when opportunities arise. Overall, current pricing significantly undervalues the Fund’s resilient cash flows. The forward price-to-earnings ratio below 12 times appears unreasonably low, given that these investments are projected to grow earnings by nearly 10% annually, in our estimation.‍

‍While the outcome of geopolitical events remains uncertain, the likelihood of elevated inflation has increased due to higher global energy prices. The Fund invests in hard assets that tend to retain value and generate high-margin, recurring cash flows. By focusing on rental revenues, which are tied directly or indirectly to inflation, the Fund seeks to protect returns against erosion of purchasing power. Additionally, the Fund’s underlying investments use conservative debt levels, which help maintain stability during periods of inflation and provide transaction optionality.

‍Activity

Proceeds from the successful privatizations of Mandarin Oriental and National Storage Australia REIT, mentioned in our last shareholder letter, were used to initiate two new investments. With discounted valuations and attractive potential entry points across the Fund’s investment universe, Fund Management often feels ‘spoilt for choice’ when seeking new ideas. As such, Fund Management has remained highly disciplined in choosing new investments, favoring high-quality real estate, premium real estate management platforms, aligned management teams, and conservatively financed companies with clear catalysts for earnings growth and share price appreciation.

In this regard, Fund Management took advantage of weak market sentiment and volatility to initiate a new investment in Hang Lung Group (“Hang Lung”). The primary rationale for this investment is Hang Lung’s strong position in the premium and luxury retail property sector, its well-established ‘66’ mall brand, and its strategic presence across Hong Kong and mainland China. The company’s early and ongoing expansion into mainland China demonstrates a long-term vision, resulting in 11 strategically located malls, including a significant new opening this quarter. Despite its high-quality portfolio, Hang Lung’s shares are deeply undervalued, trading at just 22% of book value and an 8 times earnings multiple—offering investors exposure to premium assets at compelling valuations. Fund Management identifies several potential share price catalysts, including a transition to higher returns following years of development and capital expenditure intensity that is now completing. This is combined with the acceleration of Chinese luxury retail activity, with Hang Lung’s China retail sales rising 18% in 4Q 2025 and momentum continuing. Furthermore, there are several avenues to realize value: simplifying and completing the legacy residential developments, pursuing a possible merger with Hang Lung Properties, and potentially divesting assets into a managed Chinese REIT.

Valuable management platforms at deeply discounted prices are not limited to Asia. This quarter, the Fund also initiated a position in London-listed Savills Plc (“Savills”) for its established credibility, global reach, and attractive valuation. As a leading real estate services company founded in 1855, Savills offers comprehensive services across commercial, residential, and specialized property through 700 offices worldwide. The franchise's reputation for quality, particularly in Asia and the U.K., underpins its ability to attract and retain clients, supporting its long-term growth prospects and justifying the Fund’s investment rationale.

A major recent development for Savills is its anticipated acquisition of the U.S. real estate services group Eastdil Secured, which significantly strengthens its U.S. platform and creates new opportunities for revenue growth and operational synergies. The transaction is expected to close in the coming months. Despite this strengthening of its business, Savills trades at approximately 5 times EBITDA, while U.S. peers trade above 10 times. The wide valuation gap highlights a clear opportunity for share price appreciation as Savills integrates Eastdil and delivers on its growth objectives. If the market does not recognize this value, management could pursue a primary U.S. listing, a move that has unlocked higher valuations for other U.K. companies. In summary, Savills offers investors a well-established global franchise with significant potential for both operational improvement and valuation re-rating.

‍Positioning

In addition to the above, allocations for the Third Avenue International Real Estate Value Fund remain consistent with recent quarters. The Fund emphasizes investments in (i) deep value and special situations, (ii) undersupplied residential markets, (iii) self-storage and logistics platforms with growth potential, as well as (iv) mid-cap firms with specialized or non-traditional assets and (v) diversified geographic exposure, as shown in the following charts.‍

‍

‍

‍

‍

Outlook Commentary

While the long-term impact of the Iran conflict on inflation remains unclear, the Fund most recently faced higher inflation in 2022, driven by a combination of COVID-related monetary stimulus and a spike in European energy prices caused by the Russia-Ukraine conflict. At the time, European investments were negatively affected; however, active management helped the Fund's European portfolio outperform other European real estate constituents by 6% in 2022 and 9% in 2023. With the current Iran conflict in mind, the Fund has been structured to perform well during periods of elevated inflation risk and uncertain interest rates by focusing on:

  1. Low multiples and high yields - The Fund’s low 12x earnings multiples provide a wide margin of safety and strong positioning against inflation. Such low earnings multiples are associated with high real estate cap rates when implied by the share price. For example, the Fund’s self-storage investments, at the current share price, trade at implied real estate cap rates of 7.5%, compared with the U.S. average of 5.9%. That’s despite storage cash flows that should keep pace with, or even beat, inflation, and despite the Fund’s investments offering better growth prospects relative to the U.S., driven by lower occupancy, lower current rents, and more development opportunities. Similarly, the Fund’s industrial real estate investments have an implied cap rate of 7%, while U.S. industrial REITs trade at 5.6%. Similar disparities exist across the Fund’s residential, data center, and homebuilder segments.
  2. Inflation-linked and high cash conversion - Most rents in the Fund are inflation-linked or short-term, allowing quick adjustments to rising prices. For example, nearly all rents in Europe include inflation-indexation clauses, so revenues increase with the annual rate of inflation, while in Asia, most rents allow mark-to-market every few years. Consistent with the Fund’s underwriting process, investments target markets with limited supply and pricing power. Most rely on structural, long-term growth factors rather than short-term cyclical changes, such as the Fund’s focus on the global trend toward supply chain resilience and nearshoring in industrial real estate, immature self-storage, or residential real estate in undersupplied cities. Importantly, the underlying real estate assets require relatively low capital expenditures, and when combined with a preference for high operating margins, this results in a high conversion of revenue to free cash flow.
  3. Low debt levels — The Fund’s conservative balance sheets, with low debt levels and a mix of fixed- and floating-rate debt, help limit the impact of rising interest rates on cash flow. On average, the Fund’s debt as a proportion of assets, or ‘loan-to-value ratio3,’ is about 20%. In addition, Fund Management prefers highly flexible balance sheets with high cash balances, access to credit facilities, and multiple funding sources, so financial flexibility throughout the cycle can provide optionality for potential value-add transactions.

The combination of high implied yields (low multiples), inflation-linked revenues with high cash conversion, and low debt levels with financing flexibility positions the Fund well for an inflationary environment. Trading at what Fund Management views as the most attractive valuations in the Fund’s history (valuation as measured by a 12 times P/E ratio and a 37% discount to net asset value), we are constructive about the return potential. At the same time though, Fund Management remains alert to further volatility stemming from the Iran conflict, which could create dislocation and attractive investment opportunities. While the Fund's pipeline of prospective investments is as strong as it has been in several years, Fund Management intends to remain disciplined and opportunistic.

We sincerely appreciate your continued trust and support. Please reach out to realestate@thirdave.com with any questions, comments, or ideas. We look forward to keeping you informed and partnering with you for continued success in the coming quarter and beyond.

Sincerely, The Third Avenue Real Estate Value Team

Quentin Velleley, CFA Portfolio Manager

data.title: Celestica Q1 2026: The Beat-And-Raise The Market Sold
data.description:
Celestica (CLS) reported Q1 2026 earnings on April 27 after the close. The company beat its own adjusted EPS guide, lifted the full-year 2026 outlook by $2.0B in revenue and $1.40 in adjusted EPS, and put a number on 2027 for the first time. The stock dropped 17% in pre-market the next morning. That gap, between what the print said and what the price did, is the article.

The business in 60 seconds

Celestica is a Toronto-based electronic manufacturing services and original-design manufacturer that spent the last five years deliberately walking away from low-margin commodity volumes (the legacy Cisco (CSCO) relationship was wound down around 2020) and reorienting around hyperscaler design partnerships. The Connectivity & Cloud Solutions (CCS) segment, which serves hyperscaler data centres, is 80% of revenue. Inside CCS sits Hardware Platform Solutions (HPS), the higher-value unit where Celestica co-engineers networking and compute reference platforms with the largest cloud buyers. HPS revenue hit $1.7B in Q1, up 63% y/y, and is 42% of total company revenue. Advanced Technology Solutions (ATS) covers aerospace and defence, healthtech, industrial, and capital equipment, and accounts for the other 20%.

The thesis

Mix shift is the business now, not a story. A year ago HPS was around 30% of revenue. Today it is 42% and rising, with Q1 revenue of $1.7B (+63% y/y) outpacing every other line in the business. HPS programs are sticky for structural reasons: they are typically single-sourced, customer-funded at the development stage, and require Celestica to act as the customer's procurement engine for scarce silicon, optics, and power components. Once a hyperscaler qualifies a reference platform with CLS, displacing it costs more than it saves.

The two design-led program wins announced this quarter are both HPS: a 1.6T co-packaged optics Ethernet switch using 1.6 Terabit switch silicon and liquid cooling, ramping in 2027 with a hyperscaler customer; and a scale-up networking switch built for AMD's Helios rack-scale architecture, which uses Ultra Accelerator Link over Ethernet to connect AMD Instinct MI450 GPUs and ships in late 2026. These are not the kind of programs traditional EMS contract assemblers win.

Hyperscaler AI capex is structural, with visibility extending into 2028. Hyperscaler data-centre capex stepped from roughly $2B in 2004 to $197B in 2024 and is on track to clear $200B annually through this decade. The networking layer is the load-bearing piece: every generation of GPUs needs the next generation of switches to avoid bandwidth becoming the bottleneck. CLS rode the 400G ramp into majority hyperscaler share, then transitioned every 400G customer to 800G with even higher share, and is now running ahead on 1.6T with multiple customer awards. The migration cycle (400G → 800G → 1.6T → 3.2T) is the company's structural advantage: switching to a different supplier mid-generation costs years of co-design, not just a price negotiation.

In the Q1 2026 release, the CEO said the 2027 outlook continues to strengthen "supported by new program wins as well as improved forecast visibility with our customers." The company disclosed 10 active 1.6T networking programs ramping in 2027, with mass production starting in H2 2026 on the first two. The 2027 capex placeholder of approximately $1.5B and the new CPO program ramping in 2027 underscore an order book extending past 2026 into 2028.

Operating leverage is showing up in the margin line. Q1 adjusted operating margin printed 8.0%, a company record, +30 bps q/q and +90 bps y/y. Adjusted ROIC reached 49.8%, +1,830 bps y/y. The 2026 EPS guide moved up faster than revenue (+16% vs. +12%).

Capital allocation has been disciplined, even through the build-out. Celestica generated $1.3B of cumulative FCF from 2019 to 2025...

... and returned roughly $1B to shareholders via buybacks over that window...

...taking the share count down ~16%.

2025 FCF was $458M, and despite the $1B 2026 capex plan and a placeholder $1.5B for 2027, the company entered Q1 with net debt of $341M and leverage of 0.6x. The yesterday-announced $2.5B credit facility provides the capacity to fund the build-out without diluting equity. The trade for the next 12-18 months is FCF margin compression (2026 FCF margin compresses from 3.7% to 2.6% on the new $19B base) in exchange for the order book that the build-out unlocks.

Q1 2026 results

Revenue of $4.05B was +53% y/y and +$392M sequentially, at the midpoint of the $3.85B-$4.15B guide. Adjusted EPS of $2.16 came in above the high end of the $1.95-$2.15 guide, beating consensus by $0.08 and rising 80% y/y. GAAP EPS was $1.83, down $0.48 sequentially.

The decline is almost entirely tax timing as the GAAP effective rate normalised from 11% in Q4 2025 to 17% in Q1 2026. Adjusted operating earnings of $325.2M generated the 8.0% margin record.

CCS revenue of $3.24B grew 76% y/y. Communications was +69% on 800G program ramps. Enterprise was +101%, slightly below the high-teens guide on a component supply constraint affecting an AI/ML compute program; the company said the constraint has since been resolved with catch-up shipments expected in subsequent quarters. ATS revenue of $806M was flat y/y but ahead of the low-single-digit-decline guide, with margin up 100 bps to 6.0%. Capex stepped to $230M (5.7% of revenue) versus $37M a year ago; FCF was $138M. Net debt closed Q1 at $341M, leverage 0.6x. The credit facility was upsized to $2.5B with the revolver moving from $750M to $1.75B and maturity extended to 2031.

Forward guidance: Q2 2026 revenue $4.15B-$4.45B, adjusted EPS $2.14-$2.34, adjusted operating margin 8.0% at the midpoint.

The 2026 outlook moved from $17.0B to $19.0B in revenue, $8.75 to $10.15 in adjusted EPS, and 7.8% to 8.1% in adjusted operating margin. The $500M FCF guide held flat to absorb the unchanged $1B capex plan.

The press release frames 2027 growth as "significantly more than" the +$6.5B growth expected in 2026, which implies a 2027 floor around $25.5B against today's $19B base, +35% on top of +53%.

The risk

Customer concentration materialised, and worse. Three customers each accounted for at least 10% of Q1 revenue: 35%, 15%, and 15%, totalling 65%. A year ago the same three were 28%, 13%, and 10%, totalling 51%. The single largest moved from 28% to 35% in twelve months. The offset is that the 35% customer funds the AMD Helios and digital-native rack-scale builds, both under non-cancellable, non-returnable contracts that protect the order book through demand swings. But concentration is concentration, and any single-customer pause is a much bigger event now than a year ago.

Component supply is a second-order risk: shortages are tightening across custom silicon, memory, 40+ layer PCBs, power components, and optical components, and the company has indicated the new $19B guide already factors these constraints in.

SA Quant Ratings

The Quant scorecard maps cleanly to the variant perception. Valuation grades D+, with every multiple at a premium to sector and to Celestica's own 5-year average. P/E Non-GAAP TTM is 60.14 against a sector median of 23.73; EV/EBITDA TTM is 39.46 against 19.85. The PEG ratio at 0.60 (B-) is the only valuation read that breaks the pattern, which is the point: the multiple is high because the growth is higher.

Growth grades A-, with forward revenue growth of 36.47% (241% above sector) and forward EPS diluted growth of 49.64%.

Profitability grades B+ and has improved for two consecutive quarters, with ROE 40.49% (A+), ROA 11.54% (A), and asset turnover 1.88x against a sector 0.61x.

Momentum grades A on a 374% one-year run; the grade softened from A+ as the post-earnings drop digested.

Revisions grade B+, and consensus EPS has marched up across all three forward years.

My verdict

The post-earnings drop priced in nothing the print confirmed and ignored the disclosure that mattered most: the implied $25.5B 2027 revenue floor. Three quarters of EPS beats in a row, two full-year raises in 90 days, design-led program wins extending into 2028, and operating leverage running ahead of plan. The bear case is concentration, and concentration matters. Everything else is sentiment. I remain bullish, with the caveat that any position in CLS requires position-sizing discipline given the volatility around prints.

data.title: ArriVent BioPharma: A First Assessment
data.description:
Shares of cancer therapy concern ArriVent BioPharma, Inc. (AVBP) have rallied roughly 75% since August 2025, in anticipation of positive results from a pivotal trial treating EGFR mutant NSCLC in mid-26. However, Dizal Pharmaceuticals just trumpeted success in a Phase 3 trial for the same EGFR exon 20 insertion mutation indication for its approved therapy Zegfrovy, although details were lacking. With the global market for exon 20 insertion mutation NSCLC likely less than $1 billion and a Phase 3 trial commencing for the slightly larger PACC mutation indication, ArriVent merited a deeper dive. A first analysis around ArriVent BioPharma follows below.

Company Overview:

ArriVent Biopharma, Inc. is a Newton Square, Pennsylvania based clinical-stage biopharmaceutical concern focused on in-licensing cancer medicines predominantly from China with the aim of commercializing them in the U.S. and other western markets. The company is advancing two programs through the clinic, including late-stage asset firmonertinib against non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). ArriVent was formed in 2021 and went public in 2024, raising net proceeds of $183.4 million at $18 per share. The stock trades for just under thirty bucks a share, translating to a market cap of approximately $1.35 billion.

Pipeline:

Firmonertinib. Through the company’s approach, it now controls the ex-greater China rights to four programs and the worldwide rights to one other, although three are currently in the preclinic. Its most important candidate is firmonertinib, an oral, highly brain-penetrant epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) mutant-selective tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) that is being investigated in multiple studies as a treatment for EGFR mutated NSCLC.

EGFR mutations (EGFRm) will present in approximately one-quarter of the nearly 200,000 Americans projected to be afflicted with NSCLC in 2026. There are several types of EGFRm, with ~70% known as classical, which include exon 19 deletions and exon 21 L858R mutations. AstraZeneca’s (AZN) third-generation EGFR TKI and mega-blockbuster Tagrisso (osimertinib) has become the clear standard of care [SOC] against classical EGFRm NSCLC, having generated sales of $7.3 billion in 2025 – although others have been sanctioned for this indication. However, it is not very effective against (nor approved for) the uncommon ones, including Exon 20 insertions (9%) and P-loop and-alpha-c-helix compressing [PACC] mutations (12%), the former of which is typically treated with a combination of chemotherapy and Johnson & Johnson’s (JNJ) injectable bispecific EGF receptor-directed and MET receptor-directed antibody Rybrevant (amivantamab).

The competitive landscape may change in Exon 20 insertions with the emergence of Chinese concern and AstraZeneca spinoff Dizal Pharmacutical’s oral, EGFR TKI Zegfrovy (sunvozertinib). On March 23, 2026, Dizal reported success in a Phase 3 trial treating NSCLC with EGFR exon 20 insertion mutation in a first-line (1L) setting with Zegfrovy monotherapy. Details were lacking, with the Chinese biotech stating that its therapy had achieved “statistically significant and clinically meaningful improvement in progression free survival [PFS] over platinum-containing chemo doublet” in a Phase 3 trial (WU-KONG 28). Having already received accelerated approval in a 2L setting from the FDA in July 2025, these results – assuming they match the verbiage – will likely guarantee full approval for Zegfrovy. The global markets ex-greater China for Exon 20 insertion and PACC mutation NSCLC indications are ~31,000 and ~42,000 patients, respectively.

Firmonertinib was in-licensed from Shanghai Allist Pharmaceuticals in 2021, the same year it is approved as Ivesa in China as a first-line (1L) therapy against classical EGFRm NSCLC after delivering statistically significant efficacy against first-generation EGFR TKI gefitinib, including confirmed CNS metastases specific objective response rate [ORR] of 91% versus 65% for gefitinib across a 358-patient trial (FURLONG). Allist was also conducting a (still ongoing) Phase 1b study (FAVOUR) in which firmonertinib reduced tumor sizes in seven of ten patients with EGFR exon 20 insertion mutations. These developments compelled ArriVent to onboard the therapy. To date, firmonertinib monotherapy in 1L setting produced a partial response [PR] in 79% (22/28) patients and initial interim median PFS (mPFS) of 10.7 months.

Due in some measure to the likely inability to demonstrate clear superiority over Tagrisso in classical and its seemingly clear advantage over SOC in uncommon EGFRm NSCLCs, ArriVent is pursuing the latter with firmonertinib stateside. Approximately one-third of NSCLCs metastasize to the brain, rendering the candidate’s brain-penetrating ability critical.

Armed with this knowledge and learnings from FAVOUR, ArriVent entered firmonertinib monotherapy into a 398-patient global Phase 3 trial (FURVENT) treating non-squamous locally advanced or metastatic NSCLC with EGFR exon 20 insertion mutation in a 1L setting. Patients were randomized 1:1:1 to receive either 160mg firmonertinib once a day, a 240mg daily dosage, or chemotherapy. Primary endpoint is progression free survival [PFS] with overall survival (OS), ORR, and duration of response (DOR) amongst the key secondary endpoints. With enrollment completed in 1Q25, pivotal data from FURVENT is anticipated in mid-2026. For this indication, firmonertinib has received Breakthrough Therapy and Orphan designations from the FDA.

Also, the company has commenced enrollment of patients in a 480-patient Phase 3 trial (ALPACCA) evaluating firmonertinib monotherapy treating 1L EGFR PACC mutant NSCLC. This study comes on the heels of data from the 1L PACC cohort of another Phase 1b study (FURTHER), in which the 240mg dose of the therapy demonstrated mPFS of 16.0 months, median DOR of 14.6 months, best ORR of 82% (confirmed ORR 68%), and a disease control rate [DCR] of 100% in PACC patients (n=22). Both doses (the other being 160mg) achieved complete CNS responses in 41% (7/17) of PACC patients with brain metastasis. ArriVent characterized firmonertinib’s safety profile as manageable with 22% of all participants in FURTHER (n=158) experiencing a Grade 3 adverse event. No Grade 4 or 5 events occurred. Patients in ALPACCA with will be randomized 1:1 to receive either 240mg firmonertinib or investigators choice of therapy (either Tagrisso or Boehringer Ingelheim’s kinase inhibitor Gilotrif (afatinib)). Primary endpoints are ORR and PFS. With the trial just beginning enrollment, no timeline for data has been forwarded.

Beyond these later-stage trials, ArriVent intends to participate in (or run a parallel) Phase 1b study investigating firmonertinib as an adjuvant therapy in other EGFR uncommon mutations in China (or stateside).

In return for the ex-greater China global rights to firmonertinib, the company paid Allest $40 million cash and 1.28 million shares of stock upfront. ArriVent is potentially obligated on up to $110 million of development milestones (of which it has paid $5 million to date) and up to $655 million in commercial milestones. Allest is also eligible to receive high single digits to low mid-teens royalties.

ARR-217. The company’s other clinical asset is ARR-217, a CDH17-targeting antibody drug conjugate [ADC] treating gastrointestinal cancers, which was in-licensed from Chinese concern Lepu Biopharma in January 2025. It is undergoing investigation in a dose escalation Phase 1 study treating patients with unresectable locally advanced or metastatic solid tumors. Results are anticipated in 2H26. Lepu is entitled to upfront and near-term milestones totaling $47 million, up to $1.16 billion of development, regulatory, and commercial milestones, as well as high single digit to low-teen royalties.

Beyond firmonertinib and ARR-217, ArriVent has in-licensed three other ADCs with solid tumor prospect ARR-002 expected to enter the clinic in 2H26.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

To pay for the advancement of its in-licensed candidates, the company held cash and equivalents of $312.8 million on December 31, 2025, providing it an operating runway into 2H27. It has an undrawn $75 million credit facility, of which $40 million becomes available upon the achievement of certain milestones. No money has been withdrawn to date.

As it awaits pivotal data from FURVENT, the Street is unanimously optimistic on ArriVent’s outlook, as can be seen above. On average, sell-side analysts see firmonertinib hitting the market in 2H27 with the company achieving profitability in FY29, when its therapy generates sales of $251 million.

Verdict:

Shares of AVBP rallied to an all-time high of $36.37 in November 2024 on positive early returns from firmonertinib treating EGFR PACC mutant NSCLC, but they succumbed to a news vacuum and a known need to raise additional capital, bottoming out at $15.47 during the tariff selloff in April 2025. Once its share price rebounded above $20, management pulled the trigger, diluting shareholders via a secondary offering in July 2025, raising net proceeds of $80.5 million at $19.50 per share.

Make no mistake, poor results would be catastrophic for ArriVent and ALPACCA is not likely to produce meaningful results for several years, and now a further layer of uncertainty has been added with Dizal’s successful (if detail lacking) Zegfrovy trial for the same indication. Results from firmonertinib’s registrational study are somewhat de-risked, rendering outright failure highly unlikely. And with Tagrisso generating over $7 billion from ~70% of the EGFRm NSCLC pie, there is a multi-billion-dollar market opportunity for the balance, including slightly less than $1 billion globally for the Exon 20 insertion mutation indication and slightly more for PACC.

However, in light of Zegfrovy clinical news, the recommendation is to stay to the sidelines until Dizal shows its cards regarding WU-KONG 28. If Zegfrovy produced mPFS of less than 10 months, it would signal a green light to get back into ArriVent as the bet here is FURVENT will produce meaningful greater than 10 months of mPFS. If WU-KONG 28 is above 15 months, it would be a red light. Anything in between is too close to call in this uncertain market, the play is to stay on the sidelines.

data.title: Vistance Networks: What Comes After The Special Dividend (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Investment Rating Update - "Buy"

I covered Vistance Networks, Inc. (VISN) when it was still known as CommScope (the company rebranded on January 14, 2026). VISN has finally sold its Connectivity and Cable Solutions segment to Amphenol, and yesterday, we could have seen the dividend payout to shareholders, which is why the stock price closed almost 50% lower on the day.

Most people knew about the special dividend and that it would come at some point, but I assume many investors might be wondering now, “What comes next for VISN now that the dividend has already been paid?” In my opinion, VISN is likely to start its gradual recovery process from here as the remaining business keeps gaining traction. I’m not suggesting that the whole 50% move down will be recovered shortly, but for what it’s worth, and for the catalysts now embedded in their Aurora Networks (formerly Access Network Solutions) and RUCKUS Networks segments, the stock looks quite cheap to me. I expect that in the next earnings call that should take place on April 30th (pre-market, according to Seeking Alpha), the management will lay out the new development path and provide some guidance that will be enough to calm down some worrying shareholders. In my article, I’ll dedicate some time to earnings preview to assess what’s in the current consensus estimates ahead of the print. Overall, I remain bullish on VISN after yesterday's post-dividend slump.

VISN's Vision Following The Restructuring

A few words about the mechanics of yesterday's correction. Yesterday was the ex-dividend date for Vistance's massive $10.00 per share special cash distribution, so the fact that VISN lost $9.63 on the day points to a bullish momentum. The stock exchange automatically extracts the dividend sum directly from the stock price level. But right after the drop, market participants started actively buying the dip, so adjusting for the payout, VISN actually closed higher yesterday (my math shows an adjusted rise of ~1.89%).

The management used only the cash proceeds received from the CCS sale to Amphenol for the payout, and as far as I understood, they’re not going to add any new leverage to the remaining business because of the overlevered past and the overall uncertainty on the debt markets right now.

I like the execution quality of VISN’s management – the "say-do" ratio looks good. For example, when the current CEO and CFO inherited CommScope, it was a highly leveraged conglomerate that was actually struggling to integrate its past acquisitions, so they promised to deleverage the balance sheet and streamline operations. After promising, they started the execution. First, they sold Home Networks to Vantiva in January 2024 for a 24.7% equity stake and $250 million in cash. Second, they sold OWN (Outdoor Wireless Networks) and DAS (Distributed Antenna Systems) to Amphenol for $2.0 billion in net cash (closed in January 2025). Third, they sold OneCell business (again to Amphenol) for $7.4 million. And the fourth step was the well-covered sale of CCS for $10.5 billion. All these transactions took only a few months to materialize, and as a result, Vistance’s net leverage ratio of 7.8x in FY2024 turned to 4.8x (after the OWN & DAS deals), and now it’s virtually none as the management used the proceeds to completely pay off all existing third-party debt and fully redeem all of Carlyle's preferred equity.

VISN has begun a new corporate chapter, now being a pure-play edge connectivity market representative. The remaining business has two main segments - Aurora Networks (formerly Access Network Solutions) and RUCKUS Networks. So, there’s no turnaround label attached to VISN – the management just needs to keep executing as well as they did before, but now at the convergence of broadband access and enterprise wireless networking end markets. We know that in FY2025, the remaining businesses generated ~$1.93 billion in sales (+40% YoY), producing $379 million in adjusted EBITDA on that base (+176% YoY, by the way). So, the remaining business is far from being a laggard or a non-growing, unnecessary asset that Amphenol and all other players didn’t want to buy.

Aurora Networks is now the biggest segment with over 63% of total net revenues. Given that the whole cable industry is currently going through a multi-year upgrade cycle to DOCSIS 4.0 to compete with fiber-to-the-home (FTTH) providers, Aurora Networks’ top line is very likely to keep growing further, in my opinion. Aurora has already seen massive shipments of FDX (Full Duplex DOCSIS) amplifiers thanks to Comcast (CMCSA), having secured a massive new contract with a second Tier 1 North American MSO for ESD (Extended Spectrum DOCSIS) amplifiers. It’s going to begin shipping in 2026, and it might bring in tens of millions of dollars in the coming years in the form of real revenues, which is great news for those holding VISN after the special dividend cut-off.

In addition to the above, Aurora also received approval for its breakthrough "Unified Node". It is basically a single remote PHY device that supports both 1.8 GHz ESD and 1.2 GHz FDX operations, based on the description, so it can allow cable operators to reduce their inventory overhead and capital expenses. It’s essential for Aurora’s new contracts with cable operators ahead – anything that can save them money is a great selling proposition for VISN.

Back in 2024, VISN bought Casa Systems' assets for $45.1 million, and the tech underneath has been integrated so that Aurora has managed to develop a powerful virtual CMTS (vCCAP) platform. The management noted last time that they’ve secured a major deployment with Tier 1 European service providers, so I assume it’s going to add another growth layer on top of Aurora’s business ahead.

The other segment is RUCKUS. It brings in only $687 million to the table, but I also like its prospects ahead with the purpose-driven Wi-Fi development and switching networks for high density environments. The Wi-Fi 7 tech is gaining traction from the enterprise markets right now because it offers lower latency and massive bandwidth improvements, which is critical if the end users increase their workloads. RUCKUS solves the problem on its side. And they’ve already secured high-profile reference wins like the multiple U.S. professional sports stadiums and a Middle East hospital network, based on the management’s comments. RUCKUS should complete its switch from a pure hardware model to a recurring software model over the next few quarters, and once it’s done, it’s going to push up the gross margins through 2027 and 2028, with adjusted EBITDA likely hitting the low-20% range.

Previewing Vistance's Upcoming Earnings

VISN is set to report for its Q1 2026 results in April 30 after close, and it’s going to be the first quarterly print that will reflect the company's post-restructuring pure edge connectivity presence on the market. The consensus expects an EPS of $0.22 and a net revenue of $461.85 million, according to Seeking Alpha:

When the analysts asked about the outlook from the management on the Q4 earnings call, the CFO only said that there area going to be some stranded costs in 2026 that will be removed as VISN moves to 2027:

So in '26, we'll have some stranded costs. And then as we go through the year, those stranded costs will be removed. So by the time we get to '27, the stranded cost impact to CCS will be minimal, but that will be a drag for us from an EBITDA perspective in '26.

Source

On a QoQ basis, the Q4 will most likely look weaker because of those costs, but it’s critical for us to monitor a few things in the upcoming update. First, I’m interested to see how well the top line is actually holding because the CFO also guided that Q1 2026 revenue and EBITDA for the Aurora segment would decline QoQ. It’s not an operational problem but rather a seasonality adjustment. In addition, there was an unusual spike late last year as customers delayed next-gen upgrades that makes the story more complicated. So, it’s the first thing I’d pay attention to. Second, we need to see RUCKUS stabilization. The management said on the Q4 call that Q1 revenue and adjusted EBITDA for the RUCKUS segment will be in line with Q4 2025, so if there’s a higher-than-expected rise in Q1 in this segment, it’s going to be a strong catalyst for the stock. Third thing that I’d be watching is VISN’s backlog conversion. We know that the firm existed 2025 with an order backlog of $631.8 million, and so we need to see at what speed it’s being converted to actual recognized revenues.

And, of course, I expect the management team lay out the new path for VISN in the next 2-3 years. The restructuring like the one we’ve seen here is a rarity, and when it happens, the whole corporate plan usually is rewritten in full. So, we need more updates on what’s on the executives’ minds regarding Aurora and RUCKUS. Maybe there are already some new signings we should know about. And what about the new cost control? The one hidden catalyst for the next year is the elimination of stranded costs - by 2027, this cost item should drop directly to the bottom line, providing an automatic boost to EBITDA margins even if revenues remain flat, and that’s unlikely fully priced in by the consensus just yet as most people are focusing too much on the special dividend.

VISN's Valuation Setup Looks Interesting

The post-restructuring price per share is $9.90, and there are 225.5 million shares outstanding (accounting for basic shares and recent equity issuances), so there’s an implied market cap of $2.15 billion. No debt, no preferred equity on the books, so logically, there has to be a higher valuation now.

The current consensus points at $2.24 billion in sales for FY2027, so the implied forward price-to-sales ratio arrives at 0.96x. With no debt and seemingly nice prospects ahead, VISN is likely to come back to its previous 1.25-1.50x in sales multiple over the next few months, in my view.

If my assumption is right, VISN is currently undervalued by 30-56%, depending on the exit P/S multiple one applies.

Risks To My Views

All turnaround stories are risky by definition because there’s no guarantee that the turnaround proceeds as expected and in this case, all the investors’ hopes will age poorly. I’ve noted above that VISN isn’t a turnaround story anymore in my opinion, but I agree that there might be some post-turnaround corporate earthquakes if Aurora and RUCKUS fail to meet the high expectations of the market. Specifically, any delay in MSO capital expenditure cycles or pushbacks in the DOCSIS 4.0 rollout timeline will directly impact Aurora's revenues in 2026-2028, so we might see a lot of negative revisions ahead. If negatives beat positives, the stock is unlikely to come back up to its previous valuation levels. Trading at 0.8x FY2027 revenues, VISN may lose ~15% of its market cap in the coming months.

Conclusion

After the special dividend has been accounted for, VISN has become a great recovery play that is likely to return some its yesterday’s lost market cap through the current growth initiatives in the remaining businesses. I see an undervaluation of 30-56% in the next 12-24 months, which is why I remain bullish on the VISN ahead of its upcoming Q1 2026 update.

The following segment was excerpted from the Brown Advisory Small Cap Growth Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary.

The Brown Advisory Small-Cap Growth Strategy declined during the first quarter of 2026, but outperformed the Russell 2000® Growth Index, the portfolio's primary benchmark.

Quarter-to-Date Additions

REPRESENTATIVE SMALL-CAP GROWTH ACCOUNT PORTFOLIO ACTIVITY AS OF MARCH 31, 2026

  • FTI Consulting, Inc. (FCN) is a global business advisory firm that helps organizations navigate complex financial, legal, operational, and reputational challenges. It is a leading consultant in the corporate restructuring space and should stand to benefit greatly if rates stay higher for longer and we believe the company has been under-earning, as it rebuilds its economic consulting business after the departure of a few key employees over the past few years.
  • After following the company for many years, we purchased shares of Guardant Health, Inc. (GH), as recent due diligence highlighted a stable competitive environment and underscored a number of positive catalysts for the company over the next several years. Guardant Health is a leading precision oncology company that develops blood based tests for cancer screening, therapy selection, treatment monitoring, and recurrence detection, enabling data driven cancer care across the full disease continuum.
  • Knife River Corporation (KNF) is a vertically integrated producer of aggregates, asphalt, and construction services operating in the Pacific Northwest region. The company largely serves the public construction arena - building roads, bridges, and airports for federal and state customers, but is seeing rising demand from the private sector for data centers. The company emerged from a weaker demand environment in 2025 that we think should be stronger in 2026, and lead to pricing and margin growth for the company.
  • Shares of Lantheus Holdings Inc (LNTH) were added after the company received FDA approval of its key product TruVu, positively shifting the competitive positioning and allowing more confident underwriting of the operational outlook.
  • Shares of Lumexa Imaging Holdings, Inc. (LMRI) were bought following a significant pullback in valuation after the company's IPO. The company is the second largest player in the highly fragmented outpatient imaging space, which we believe will see years of growth driven by secular & demographic tailwinds.
  • We initiated a position in Q2 Holdings, Inc. (QTWO), which engages in the provision of digital banking and lending solutions to financial institutions and financial technology companies, among others, wishing to incorporate banking into their customer engagement and servicing strategies. Digital solutions are becoming more important for its customers, as AI drives demand for software to combat fraud within banks, which should help drive continued bookings momentum.
  • We used recent weakness as an opportunity to initiate a position in Talen Energy (TLN), an independent power producer whose portfolio includes the Susquehanna nuclear plant and other dispatchable generation assets, with a growing focus on serving power demand from data centers.
  • Concerns over potential share losses to oral weight loss drugs and an unexpected CEO retirement announcement sent shares of West Pharmaceutical (WST) lower, offering an attractive entry point to a company we know well.

data.title: Brown Advisory Small Cap Growth Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
The Brown Advisory Small-Cap Growth Strategy declined during the first quarter of 2026, but outperformed the Russell 2000® Growth Index, the portfolio's primary benchmark. The portfolio benefited from strong stock selection in the Information Technology and Energy sectors, while Health Care and Materials weighed on relative results.

The first quarter proved to be a volatile one, with the Russell 2000 Growth Index rallying nearly 10% to new all-time highs at the start the year, only to fall sharply into a correction shortly after, exacerbated by an escalating conflict in the Middle East. Only three sectors within the Russell 2000 Growth Index posted positive returns in the quarter, and the outperformance of the Energy sector relative to the broad Index was historically wide, marking one of the most significant periods of outperformance of a single sector in the last 40 years.

Source: Furey Research Partners as of 03/31/2026.

Updates on some of the much-discussed thematic headwinds to the portfolio from last year are included below, but regardless of what the market does, our focus remains on what we can control. The team has been working to ensure we have a robust pipeline of high-quality, durable growth companies and we have leveraged that work in this most recent bout of volatility, adding 8 new holdings while exiting 11 in the quarter. We had two additional take-outs in the first quarter, OneStream, Inc. (OS) and Mister Car Wash, Inc. (MCW), both by private equity, which is an acknowledgment that many companies are

trading below their intrinsic value today.

A Bifurcation in the “AI Trade”

We spent much of last year discussing the rally in companies that were perceived to be artificial intelligence (AI) winners, at the expense of pretty much everything else. Late last year we saw a split in the “AI Trade”, where the names within the Industrials sector continued their strong performance, while those in Information Technology fell, often sharply. That divergence continued in the first quarter, as the technology levered AI/Crypto/Quantum stocks languished and the AI beneficiaries in Industrials rallied sharply. The portfolio benefited from this divergence within Information Technology, but it remained a headwind in the Industrials sector.

The Death of Software

The software industry continues to see significant multiple compression as the market digests advancements in AI tools that threaten the moats of many companies. This narrative began last year, but the selloff accelerated in the first quarter following announcements from Anthropic, and others, that spread worry and fear throughout the software, data services and information services industries. While we have seen significant multiple contraction as a result of this pervasive narrative, the software industry still trades at a premium to its historical levels, so additional downward pressure could remain. We are cautious on the space, favoring vertically integrated companies with unique data assets, operating in niche end markets and preferably with AI tailwinds of their own.

Source: Furey Research Partners as of 03/31/2026.

Source: Furey Research Partners (FRP), FactSet®. The information provided in this material is not intended to be and should not be considered to be a recommendation or suggestion to engage in or refrain from a particular course of action or to make or hold a particular investment or pursue a particular investment strategy, including whether or not to buy, sell, or hold any of the securities mentioned. Portfolio level information is based on a representative Small-Cap Growth account and provided as Supplemental Information. Sectors are based on the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS®) classification system. The composite performance shown above reflects the Small-Cap Growth Equity Composite managed by Brown Advisory Institutional. Brown Advisory Institutional is a GIPS compliant firm and is a division of Brown Advisory LLC. Past performance is not indicative of future results. Please see the end of this presentation for a GIPS Report, important disclosures and a complete list of terms and definitions.

Quality Modestly Outperformed Following a Challenging 2025

After a challenging backdrop for higher quality last year, we have seen a pause in the low-quality rally that weighed on many active managers last year. While lower quality did underperform, we have not seen a major reversal in the relative performance. We cannot control which factors the market rewards, but we continue to have conviction in our philosophy that investing with a quality orientation in small caps will be rewarded over full market cycles.

Source: Jeffries, FactSet and Brown Advisory Analysis as of 03/31/2026.

Conclusion

Relative performance has benefited from an improving market environment, and we have continued to see strong fundamental momentum from our portfolio companies. While not until June, we have begun to analyze the upcoming Russell Reconstitution, which will significantly impact the composition of our benchmark, the Russell 2000 Growth Index. We cannot offer any predictions on the outcome, duration or collateral damage of the ongoing conflict in the Middle East, but we are working through the potential downstream impact to our portfolio companies, and what sustained higher oil prices could mean for the asset class and economy. As always, we remain focused on executing our investment philosophy and process, making the best bottom-up, fundamental and long-term investment decisions possible.

Composite Performance

Brown Advisory Institutional is a GIPS compliant firm and is a division of Brown Advisory LLC. Please see the Brown Advisory Small-Cap Growth Composite GIPS Report at the end of this presentation.

Sector Diversification

AS OF MARCH 31, 2026

  • We exited our lone position in Communication Services in the quarter, and we are meaningfully underweight the group.
  • Consumer Discretionary is a material underweight, as the health of the consumer remains a question and could be further pressured should energy price remain high.
  • Consumer Staples is represented by one sizeable, long-term holding and we are modestly underweight the group.
  • Energy remains a modest overweight, though we have been reducing exposure and now hold just one name in the group.
  • Financials are a material underweight as we remain skeptical of some “fintech” holdings in the benchmark and currently have no exposure to insurance.
  • Health Care is a meaningful underweight. We are materially underweight to the biotechnology and pharmaceuticals industries and materially overweight the life sciences tools & services industry.
  • Industrials remain a large overweight, but our holdings continue to reflect an eclectic, diverse group of businesses that are less cyclical than the small-cap benchmark’s.
  • We are relatively in line with the benchmark’s allocation to Information Technology, reducing exposure to the group this quarter as we exited some names that were acquired in recent months.
  • Materials is slightly underweight relative to the benchmark.
  • Real Estate is overweight relative to the benchmark.
  • We initiated a position in the Utilities sector this quarter, and we are now modestly overweight in the group.

The Average Weight of a position or sector refers to the daily average for the period covered in this report of a stock's value as a percentage of the portfolio.

Allocation Effect measures the impact of the decision to allocate assets differently than those in the benchmark.

Selection and Interaction Effect reflects the combination of selection effect and interaction effect. Selection effect measures the effect of choosing securities that may or may not outperform those of the benchmark. Interaction effect measures the effect of allocation and selection decisions (i.e., did we overweight the sectors in which we underperformed).

Total Effect reflects the combination of allocation, selection and interaction effects. Totals may not equal due to rounding.

The Total Return of an equity security is the sum of the return from price movement and the return due to dividend payments or other sources of income. Standard benchmark-, sector- and portfolio-level returns are the sums of the weights of each security multiplied by its return, summed and calculated daily and summed over the period covered by the report or by an otherwise-noted period.

Contribution To Return is calculated by multiplying a security's beginning weight as a percentage of a portfolio by that security's return for the period covered in the report.

Free cash flow (FCF) represents the cash a company generates after cash outflows to support operations and maintain its capital assets. Unlike earnings or net income, free cash flow is a measure of profitability that excludes the non-cash expenses of the income statement and includes spending on equipment and assets as well as changes in working capital.

Beta is a measure of portfolio volatility. It is equal to the ratio of a portfolio's volatility relative to its benchmark index's volatility over time. It is equal to the excess return of a portfolio over a risk-free investment, minus that portfolio's expected return given its volatility relative to its benchmark index.

ROE, or Return on Equity , is equal to a company's net income for a full fiscal year, divided by total shareholder equity. data.title: China Clean Technology ETF: Is CATL A Defining Energy Company Of The Electric Era?
data.description:
As the world transitions to more renewable energy, accelerated by geopolitical fault lines exposed by the closure of the Strait of Hormuz earlier this year, we believe energy storage will become as critical as oil and gas reserves. As oil majors did at the dawn of the petroleum age, CATL has emerged as a dominant supplier of a key energy input. With nearly 40% share of the global battery market,1 CATL is already the world’s largest battery supplier.

In our opinion, CATL’s current position as a major energy player on the rise is analogous to that of many US oil majors' early days in terms of scale, technological moat, access to raw materials, and value trajectory.

Scale

CATL already controls between 34% and 38% of the global electric vehicle battery market.1 Despite being based in China, CATL has achieved a truly global reach, supplying carmakers, including Tesla, BMW,1 Ford,2 and Mercedes-Benz.1 This mirrors the pattern of major US oil companies, such as Chevron and Exxon Mobil, establishing partnerships and refining assets in global markets.

CATL is also moving beyond just vehicle batteries to providing energy storage systems for electrical utilities around the world, greatly increasing its potential scale. This is reminiscent of when John D. Rockefeller's Standard Oil moved from mainly providing kerosene for lamps and stoves to providing oil to heat entire homes.

Average annual electricity consumption worldwide is expected to triple as a result of AI use cases. The only recent comparable event was the widespread adoption of air conditioning, but that only doubled electricity consumption, according to estimates.

CATL is already well-positioned to help support this massive potential strain on global electrical grids. The company is beginning to provide highly efficient, large-scale energy storage systems to public utilities in China for use in reducing grid load during peak periods.9 It has also begun to supply such grid-oriented storage systems to local automaker Ford for use with US utility companies.8

Technological Moat

CATL has proven capable of continued innovation in battery technology. In 2024, it was the first to commercialize a vehicle battery that, according to marketing, could charge nearly 250 miles of range in 10 minutes.4 In April, CATL announced a key breakthrough in sodium-ion battery technology that promises to lower costs over traditional lithium-based batteries, improve performance at lower temperatures, and further speed up charging times.7 Achieving such efficiency makes CATL an exceedingly attractive partner for global automakers looking to reduce potential buyers’ range anxiety. We believe this technological moat has enabled CATL to steadily increase its margins over the past three years, despite chronic vehicle oversupply in its home market, China.

This mirrors the pattern of Exxon and other American oil companies essentially selling their refining and extraction expertise to less advanced firms, as Exxon’s predecessor did with Saudi Aramco in 1948.5

Access to Raw Inputs

Batteries require unique inputs, including lithium, cobalt, and other rare-earth elements (REEs). CATL benefits from its base in China, which has considerably better access to REEs compared to many other major economies.6 Moreover, CATL is now seeking to vertically integrate with the purchase of crucial mining assets. Similarly, during their rise, US oil majors often benefited from their proximity to plentiful underground oil in West Texas.

Value Trajectory

CATL’s market capitalization has surged over 600% in the past five years to reach a level that is on par with many of the world’s largest oil companies.

CATL's value may still increase as it expands the scope of its energy storage technology to include use in electrical grids. Ford's attempt to leverage battery plants in Michigan and Kentucky that already use CATL's technology to supply energy storage solutions for electrical grids,8 if successful, may encourage other carmakers around the world to do the same, leading to increased revenues for CATL. Meanwhile, the increased use of weather-dependent renewables, such as wind and solar energy, may also drive increased demand for energy storage. Similarly, new markets for automobiles, plastics, and modern heating and cooling technology have contributed significantly to oil companies' revenue growth in recent decades.

How to Invest

data.title: XP: Growth Is Improving, But Retail Flows Are Still Missing
data.description:
XP Inc.'s (XP) investment thesis remains broadly intact from my previous on-the-stock coverage, but with a more nuanced near-term outlook.

As I highlighted in a previous article, XP's bull case was never euphoric, but grounded in improving fundamentals such as (1) resilient Net New Money; (2) strong fee-based growth; and (3) a high ROE even with unfavorable mix scenarios driven by macro cycles. All this is supported by a still undemanding valuation.

At present, the framework still holds, but with the addition that execution is improving and margins expanding, driven more by mix and operational leverage than by volume, which makes the thesis more cyclical than ideal. The lack of acceleration in net inflows suggests that demand still needs to normalize and also reinforces the view of XP as a leveraged play on a future cycle rather than a current one.

Over the long term, the asymmetry between earnings potential (driven by operational leverage) and current valuation makes the thesis attractive for long-term oriented investors and supports my Buy rating. In the short term, however, the path to unlocking it still depends on a normalization in flows and retail activity, which can make the journey quite volatile.

A Rapid Recap

In its last print, posted in February of this year, XP's 4Q25 results showed clear improvements in its consolidated performance, but still with a "mixed quality".

For example, XP posted revenues of +12% YoY and in FY25 a total of +8%. EBT grew 20% YoY in the quarter, and the EBT margin stood at a robust 31.3% (already at the top of the guidance). These three indicators already show efficiency gains, but by mix and operating leverage, not volume.

XP makes it clear that behind this improvement, mix is the real driver. Although retail still forms ~75% of the mix, the increase in revenues from Corporate & Issuer Services (most profitable areas), rising from 13% to ~14%-17%, reinforces the thesis around quality. At the same time, retail not accelerating lays bare the lack of trading tailwinds, limiting more robust short-term upside.

In terms of operational leverage, XP managed to grow SG&A by +10%, down from +12% revenue growth in 4Q25, and contributed to stable efficiency at 34.7%, showing that it is finally starting to capture the investments made in tech and scale over the years. Now, on equity quality, the scenario is not ideal, but also nothing catastrophic. Assets Under Custody (AUC) grow for two reasons: Net New Money (NNM) (new money coming in) and market performance (assets rising). In the present case of XP, AUC jumped 22% YoY in 4Q25, but at the same time, NNM was flat/falling, clearly showing that the growth came much more from the market than from new demand. This raises a yellow flag around the sustainability of equity growth. If the growth comes from market performance, this puts XP very exposed to the cycle, and the current growth may not fit as "repeatable".

Therefore, flat NNM is the clearest indication that appetite remains weak in the current cycle, as interest rates are still high and retail is still avoiding risk.

But at a broader angle, calling XP's current setup bearish would be an exaggeration. The improvement in execution, mix, and lukewarm demand at the very least reveals the company in an "in-between state". This leads me to believe that there is much more of an environmental problem than necessarily a company problem. XP's share price poor performance since 4Q25 for me is the best illustration of this.

XP Is Positioned for the Cycle, but Not Benefiting Yet

Much of what is behind XP shares' solid year-to-date performance has to do with the improvement in macro expectations in Brazil.

And this is consistent with flat results in NNM. It is extremely complicated for a financial institution like XP to capture more money in an interest rate scenario (Selic) at 14.75% and a long curve at ~9.2%.

When we look at the interest rate in Brazil, the recent cut in March by 25 bps for the first time since the Selic reached 15% in July last year was the signal that, quite possibly, the worst of the monetary tightening policy is over. This largely explains the rebound in Brazilian equities over the last few months. Even better, this coincided with inflation, currently at 4.14% per year, staying within the tolerance of the Central Bank of Brazil's target.

The expectation that, with inflation stabilized, new Selic cuts will be the way throughout 2026, this expands multiples and anticipates flow, as the market starts to anticipate the return of retail in trading and inflows. And this makes XP a good leveraged play on Brazilian capital markets.

Within this scenario, XP's management team made it very evident in the 4Q25 earnings call that the recent rally in risk assets is being driven by foreign capital influx, there was no relevant increase in retail volumes, and it is still "too soon" to see this reflected in revenue.

This confirms that XP is not betting on a near-term improvement in the cycle, also saying that NNM should remain flat in the short term with ~R$20 billion per quarter following the expected level, without acceleration. It does seem a little disappointing to me that even with inflation relatively under control in Brazil and the expectation of cuts, there is still a lack of conviction for retail to re-accelerate.

Having said that, XP estimates that FY26 will still be higher than FY25, with revenue growth among mid/high teens (something around ~17% YoY), but with a critical detail that they are not counting on an improvement in take rate or market.

In other words, growth will again be driven by execution, new products, and Corporate & Issuer Services. The latter, in fact, is where XP believes it will be the main driver, with growth being sustainable in FY26.

In my opinion, this is quite bullish because it is essentially an "escape valve" for XP to grow regardless of the retail cycle, and with a better margin.

XP has worked hard to build an ecosystem where it integrates retail distribution, market making, and investment banking, which allows it to operate throughout the chain and minimize the cyclicality of the business.

Valuation Looks Cheap, but Growth Still Needs to Prove Itself

As XP is scheduled to post its 1Q26 results in mid-May, the management team recognizes that it is still too early to see an improvement in volumes, as retail activity is reaccelerating. This points to a lukewarm 1Q26 in terms of flow.

Some specific inflows, such as the liquidation of Banco Master in Brazil, generated a strong retention of funds and a significant inflow of money from this event. This can inflate the NNM in Q1 and give a "technical boost" to the numbers, but nothing structural.

That being said, with the good prospects in corporate for FY26, the market works with a projection of +25.3% YoY in EPS for 1Q26, a percentage ~12% above what the market anticipated three months ago.

At the same time, revenues are estimated to grow +25.4% YoY, about 9% above what was anticipated a quarter ago.

In my interpretation, these upward revisions are a reflection of the market assuming strong DCM, credit growing, and cross-selling continuing—as XP said is sustainable. But perhaps this is where the risk lies, where this can be more cyclical than it seems.

To a certain extent, it seems to me that the market is implicitly pricing in a macro scenario of falling interest rates and retail returning, and this may be hasty in the near-term.

At the same time, in the long-term, XP seems dirt-cheap to me when we look at multiple valuations embedded in the company's operating leverage perspective, which foresees EPS long-term growth (3-5 years CAGR) at 15.6%. Incorporating these expectations into the ~9x forward earnings that XP trades, this translates to a PEG of a mere 0.57.

The caveat here, and the biggest risk I see for the bullish thesis today, is that PEG assumes sustainable and relatively predictable growth. In XP's case, part of the growth comes from AUC (market performance), mix (corporate), and buybacks (EPS).

So I would say that the "E" of the P/E ratio may not grow in the way that PEG assumes. But even assuming a long-term growth of ~10%, PEG would be roughly 0.9.

Therefore, I would assume based on the next twelve months that a good execution, a still slow cycle, and a more conservative EPS CAGR of ~10% would deserve a fair P/E of 10-11x, which in the mid-term, assuming ~$2.18 in EPS, would result in a price target of $22 to $24 per share.

Also, a bull case with ~15% long-term EPS growth with multiple that can expand to 12x and a price target of $26 to $28 per share.

The Bottom Line

Although I have a more cautious approach in the short term due to XP trading as a leverage proxy for the Brazilian equity market, in the long term, I remain bullish on the thesis, anticipating price asymmetry in the face of consistent operational leverage.

data.title: First Financial Finally Proves It's Worth Banking On (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
On April 28th, shares of First Financial Corporation (THFF) ticked up 0.8% after management announced financial results covering the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Even though revenue fell short of what analysts anticipated, earnings per share came in slightly above what had been hoped for. The company continues to show growth in the areas that matter most. And asset quality remains strong. However, there are certain concerns that I do have. For instance, the company is not quite as cheap as I would like it to be. This is even after factoring in a really great 2025 fiscal year. On top of this, certain credit quality metrics for the company have worsened. Even with these issues factored in, I would argue that the stock is worthy, just barely, of upgrading it from the 'hold' I had it at previously to a soft 'buy'.

Taking stock of First Financial Corporation

According to the management team at First Financial Corporation, the company operates as the largest bank in Vigo County, Indiana. It has 6 full service banking branches in that county. On top of this, it operates 15 other full service banking branches throughout the state, on top of 24 branches in Illinois, 16 in Kentucky, 15 in Tennessee, and 3 in Georgia. The enterprise also has 8 loan production offices split evenly between Indiana and Tennessee. All combined, this makes it a pretty sizable enterprise and the fact that it has a market capitalization of $1.55 billion is not surprising to me.

As I mentioned at the start of this article, management announced financial results covering the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year. During that time, net interest income came in at $54.4 million. That compared nicely to the $50 million reported a year earlier. For context, this refers to net interest income that is net of provisions for credit losses as opposed to the headline net interest income reading. One driver behind this improvement ended up being a rise in the firm's net interest margin from 4.11% to 4.23%. The picture would have been better had it not been for the fact that its provision for credit losses increased from $2 million to $2.6 million.

Non-interest income also increased, growing from $10.5 million to $11.2 million. This was in spite of the fact that service charges and fees on deposit accounts contracted from $7.6 million to $7.4 million, and was driven in large part by a $0.7 million bargain purchase gain that the company booked. Despite this improvement and the rise in net interest income, overall revenue for the company of $68.1 million fell short of what analysts anticipated to the tune of $1.5 million. Naturally, this is a disappointment and would normally justify a drop in the share price instead of an increase. However, there was one offsetting factor here.

That was the fact that earnings per share for the business came in above what analysts anticipated. The belief was that the company would have generated earnings per share of $1.64 compared to the $1.55 reported the year prior. But actually, earnings per share came in at $1.67. This translated to net profits of $19.8 million compared to the $18.4 million that the business reported for the first quarter of 2025. But it is worth noting that this is part of a larger trend for the company. In 2025, for instance, management booked net profits of $79.2 million. That compared favorably to the $47.3 million reported the year prior. And a lot of that was the result of net interest margin growing from 3.71% to 4.29%.

In analyzing any bank, a discussion of the balance sheet is imperative. For the first quarter of 2026, First Financial Corporation reported deposits of $4.84 billion. That compared to the $4.55 billion that the institution had at the end of 2025. Often, an increase of this magnitude in such a short window of time is the result of a bank taking on additional high cost brokered deposits. However, according to management, this was actually attributable to the fact that, on March 1st of this year, the business acquired CedarStone Financial, a bank based out of Tennessee that the company ultimately paid $25 million for. This was responsible for bringing $313 million of deposits onto its books. Because of this change and because management did not provide additional detail, we don't know how much of its deposit base is currently classified as uninsured. But we do know that the number at the end of 2025 was 19%. It's unlikely to be materially different than it is now. And seeing as how that is still reasonably lower than the 30% maximum I typically like to see, it's unlikely that there is much to worry about on that front.

As deposits increased, the value of loans grew also. They went from $4.01 billion in 2025 to $4.37 billion in the first quarter of this year. Again, this was largely thanks to the aforementioned acquisition, which added $292 million of loans to its portfolio. This is not to say that the institution did not see any organic growth. In fact, on a linked quarter basis, organic loans increased by $77 million thanks to greater activity when it came to the commercial construction and development, commercial real estate, and consumer auto loan categories. Securities increased slightly, growing from $1.15 billion to $1.17 billion. But cash and cash equivalents contracted from $130.4 million to $96.9 million. And finally, debt did rise, expanding from $480.7 million to $558.5 million.

If we use the financial performance that First Financial Corporation achieved in 2025, we can see in the chart above that the institution is trading at a price to earnings multiple of 10.1. That's just a hair higher than the 10 maximum that I typically like to see. Relative to other similar banks, it's also trading a bit on the cheap end of the spectrum. Only one of the five institutions that I included in that chart happen to be cheaper than it is. The same ranking holds true when it comes to the price to book and price to tangible book multiples. In the chart below, you can see precisely what I mean here. Our prospect is not exactly cheap when it comes to these metrics. But it's far from expensive.

Asset quality is also one thing that the institution really has going for it. In the first chart below, you can see a return on assets reading of 1.35%. That's above the 1% minimum that I typically look for. However, relative to these other institutions, First Financial Corporation really is not exceptional. In that chart, you can see that only one of the five firms is worse off than it is on this basis. Unfortunately, the same ranking does hold true here when it comes to return on equity. In the subsequent chart below, you can see what I mean here. The reading of 11.93% is at least well above the 10% minimum that I like to see. So in that respect, the institution passes. This is not to say that I like everything when it comes to its assets. One credit quality metric that is concerning is the percentage of loans that were classified as non-performing. This figure increased from 0.26% last year to 0.64% this year. That puts the company above the 0.50% maximum that I typically like to see. But that on its own is not a deal breaker. Instead, it's just something that investors should continue to monitor.

Takeaway

Based on the data that's available right now, I would argue that First Financial Corporation remains an interesting prospect. And in fact, because of the growth that we have seen on the bottom line and the continued expansion of its balance sheet, I feel comfortable upgrading it from a 'hold' to a soft 'buy'.
